text: laparoscopic mesh rectopexy in recurrent rectal prolapse. A seventy-six year old female patient with four previous vaginal deliveries presented with a full thickness complete rectal prolapse and myofecal incontinence. She had previously undergone two perineal coloprotectomy procedures with the Almayer technique with early recurrence at six months each time. Preoperative endoscopy excluded malignancy as well as adolicosigmoid. In this scenario, we decided to perform an elective laparoscopic mesh rectopexy with the Orloid technique.

laparoscopic mesh rectopexy in recurrent rectal prolapse. A seventy-six year old female patient with four previous vaginal deliveries presented with a full thickness complete rectal prolapse and myofecal incontinence. She had previously undergone two perineal coloprotectomy procedures with the Almayer technique with early recurrence at six months each time. Preoperative endoscopy excluded malignancy as well as adolicosigmoid. In this scenario, we decided to perform an elective laparoscopic mesh rectopexy with the Orloid technique.

The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg with trocars placed, including a 5mm port in the left lower quadrant. The surgeon begins the procedure by opening the medial peritoneum above the promontory, exposing the area for mesh fixation. The left ureter and superior rectal artery are identified, and careful dissection is performed near the rectum to avoid ureter injury. Anterior dissection is challenging due to fibrotic scarring from previous surgeries extending from the pouch of Douglas to the rectovaginal septum.

The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg position with trocars placed as shown. An accessory 5mm port is used in the left lower quadrant. The surgeon begins the procedure by making a medial peritoneal opening above the promontory, exposing the area for mesh fixation. The left ureter and superior rectal artery are identified.

The surgeon carefully dissects the ureter, staying close to the rectum to prevent injury while dividing the left side of the peritoneal reflection. Anterior dissection is challenging due to a fibrotic scar extending from the pouch of Douglas to the rectovaginal septum. Additional counter-traction is applied by the second assistant from inside the vagina to help identify the correct dissection plane and reduce the risk of injury to the rectal wall and vaginal vault.

The second assistant applies counter-traction from inside the vagina to aid in identifying the dissection plane, reducing the risk of injury to the rectal wall and vaginal vault. The surgeon achieves full rectal mobilization after posterior and lateral dissection, preserving lateral stocks. Cephalic traction is applied to assess complete reduction of the prolapsed rectum. Sacral rectopexy is performed using a polypropylene mesh strip fixed with two O-proline sutures to the lateral aspect of the lower rectum and the sacrum, avoiding full thickness bites in the rectum and preventing injury to the ureters and iliac vessels.

The surgeon completes full rectal mobilization through posterior and lateral dissection, preserving lateral stocks. The second assistant applies cephalic traction to assess the complete reduction of the prolapsed rectum. Sacral rectopexy is performed using polypropylene mesh strips fixed with two O-proline sutures to the lateral aspect of the lower rectum and the sacrum, avoiding full thickness bites and preventing injury to the ureters and iliac vessels.
The surgeon closes the pelvic peritoneal opening using two absorbable V-lock running sutures to prevent contact between the mesh and the small bowel.
